topic: README best practices for technical inference repositories

sources.title: llama.cpp README
keyInsights: Hub-and-spoke pattern: README as router to specialized docs
Reproducible benchmarks with literal tool output, build hashes, and uncertainty bars
API changes surfaced before quickstart to signal breaking changes
Collapsible ecosystem sections for scalability
Explicit inclusion criteria for third-party projects with license labels

sources.title: vLLM README
keyInsights: Split features by user concern (performance vs usability)
Name mechanisms not metrics (PagedAttention + arXiv link)
One-command install with depth deferred to external docs
Explicit channel routing by inquiry type
Academic credibility via UC Berkeley origin disclosure

sources.title: SGLang README
keyInsights: Performance claims linked to versioned release blogs not inline tables
Scale as credibility (trillions of tokens, 400k+ GPUs)
Collapsible 'More' sections for news archives
Zero inline code snippets - full delegation to docs site
Adoption logos and named enterprise users for trust

sources.title: Candle README (Hugging Face)
keyInsights: Runnable example with expected output first
GPU migration shown as one-line diff
Performance framed around deployment story not benchmarks
FAQ dedicates space to build/linker errors (systems friction)
PyTorch cheatsheet as migration aid and implicit API docs

sources.title: TensorRT-LLM README
keyInsights: Formal deprecation policy (3-month migration period) for enterprise trust
Performance in dated news items not static tables
Hardware compatibility via linked support matrix
Telemetry transparency with opt-out instructions
README as marketing/navigation hub with operational complexity externalized

sources.title: mistral.rs README
keyInsights: Benchmark credibility via full versioned report with commands/model IDs
Honest about losses - includes unfavorable results vs competitors
Model support generated from loader registry to prevent drift
⭐ markers for differentiating features
Support matrix with nuanced status (In Development, Partially, Slow, Not)

sources.title: ExLlamaV2 README
keyInsights: Limitations disclosed inline with features
Archive notice at top redirecting to successor project
Concrete performance table with hardware, quantization, group size
Hedging language with performance claims
First-person candid tone builds trust

sources.title: GitHub README Official Docs
keyInsights: READMEs over 500KB are truncated
Use relative links not absolute URLs - they work in clones
Headings auto-generate table of contents
README should contain only info necessary to get started
Longer documentation belongs in wikis

sources.title: Make a README Guide
keyInsights: Essential sections: name, description, badges, visuals, installation, usage, support, roadmap, contributing, license, status
Write README early - ideally first file in project
Too long is better than too short
Use examples liberally with expected output
Validate instructions with real novice users

sources.title: Awesome README Examples
keyInsights: Visual proof over prose - GIFs and demos first
Table of contents for navigability
Badges signal project health
Collapsible sections keep long docs scannable
Philosophy/motivation sections answer 'why another X'

sources.title: ReadMe Documentation Best Practices
keyInsights: Ground docs in OpenAPI spec for accuracy
Preview before publishing
Use structured components (code blocks, callouts, tabs)
Sync with GitHub for version control
Validate as you write with linters

sources.title: PyTorch README
keyInsights: Component table upfront for massive projects
Binaries-first with interactive selector
Progressive disclosure for source builds with time estimates
Channel routing by audience (forum/Slack/issues)
Deep TOC in README as navigation index

sources.title: Rust Language README
keyInsights: README as thin stable index - all depth delegated
Source builds explicitly discouraged in README
Technical requirements framed as user benefits
Layered on-ramps for different contribution levels
Governance files at repo root

sources.title: InfluxDB README
keyInsights: Performance claims tied to specific query types
Branch-per-major-version with comparison table
Architectural facts as quick-reference spec block
Docker-verified build recipes per platform
Backward compatibility explicitly stated

sources.title: Write The Docs Beginner's Guide
keyInsights: Documentation defines what module does, not code
Writing docs improves code design
Right-size content - needed info but not too much
Plain text with version control
FAQs become outdated quickly - avoid as primary docs

sources.title: Meta Llama README
keyInsights: Gated access flow with expiring links documented
Layered legal docs (license, use policy, model card)
Model-parallelism table maps size to GPU count
Triaged reporting channels by issue type
Responsible AI documentation as first-class concern

sources.title: Triton README
keyInsights: README as contributor/developer manual for compilers
Tiered install paths by complexity
Tips for hacking section cataloging debug flags
Honest about gaps (no turnkey test runner)
User-facing tutorials outsourced to docs site

sources.title: Redis README
keyInsights: Audience-based routing with jump links
Per-OS build recipes validated against Docker images
Version pinning with rationale
Post-build smoke test with expected output
Performance claims mechanism-based not benchmark-heavy

sources.title: Standard Readme Specification
keyInsights: Required sections: title, description, TOC, background, install, usage, maintainers, contributing, license
Badges not required but aid adoption when present
Avoid badge clutter - reduces readability
README is first entry point to code
Standardization enables tooling (linters, generators)

sources.title: Art of README
keyInsights: README as cognitive funnel - broad to specific
Ideal README as short as possible without being shorter
Documentation defines module not code
Predictability saves cognitive cycles
Order by disqualification speed for honest evaluation

gaps: Limited coverage of accessibility considerations in technical docs
Minimal discussion of internationalization for global projects
Could benefit from more CI/CD integration examples for doc testing

keyTakeaways: README as router not manual - hub-and-spoke pattern with external delegation observed across all high-quality projects
Performance claims require hardware + methodology + reproducibility - unfalsifiable claims destroy credibility
Limitations disclosed inline build trust more than perfection claims - honesty is a competitive advantage
First screenful determines adoption - one-line value prop + runnable example + hardware scope above the fold
Examples must be copy-pasteable and show expected output - test in CI or they will bitrot
